name: earthscale
documentation:
# Earthscale Documentation

This is the documentation for Earthscale. Earthscale is a platform to accelerate geospatial workflows. You can log in at [https://app.earthscale.ai](https://app.earthscale.ai).

functions.name: earthscale.utils.disabled_registration_callbacks
documentation:
Context manager that temporarily disables dataset registration callbacks.

This is useful when you want to prevent automatic registration of datasets,
for example during dataset loading or copying operations.

Example:
    ```python
    with disabled_registration_callbacks():
        dataset = Dataset.load(name="my_dataset")
    ```

functions.name: earthscale.utils.generate_filter_date_range
documentation:
Generate a list of dates between start and end, with a frequency that depends on the
length of the date range such that the dates are not too dense or too sparse.

functions.name: earthscale.proj_utils.crs_from_str
documentation: Use this for Earth Engine CRS strings, since they have custom CRS definitions.

functions.name: earthscale.exceptions.convert_rasterio_to_earthscale
documentation: Handle rasterio IO errors.

classes.name: earthscale.exceptions.EarthscaleError
documentation: Base class for exceptions in this module.

classes.name: earthscale.exceptions.RasterFileNotFoundError
documentation: Raised when a file is not found

classes.name: earthscale.exceptions.RasterAccessDeniedError
documentation:
Raised when the user does not have access to the dataset,
or if the dataset is not found

classes.name: earthscale.exceptions.UnsupportedRasterFormatError
documentation: Raised when the user tries to open a raster file that is not supported

modules.name: earthscale
documentation: We're adding a noqa here as dataset needs to be imported before the DatasetRepository

methods.name: earthscale.repositories.dataset.DatasetRepository.add
documentation:
Adds a dataset to the repository. If `match_by_name` is True, the dataset is
matched by name. If a dataset with the same name exists, and is accessible by
the user, a new version of the dataset is created. If multiple datasets with the
same name exist, an error is raised. In this case, specify `match_by_name` as
False and provide a dataset id.

If `match_by_name` is False and a dataset id is specified, a new version of the
dataset is created.

In any other case, a new dataset is created.

functions.name: earthscale.cli.cli
documentation: Earthscale command line tool.

functions.name: earthscale.auth.get_gcp_billing_project
documentation:
Returns the billing project to be used for GCP billing

On the client this would be set to a cloud project the client has access to. On our
 infrastructure, this should point to the project we're running in.

functions.name: earthscale.auth.get_google_credentials
documentation:
Returns the Google credentials and the project ID

Credentials will have a valid token if the credentials are expired.

functions.name: earthscale.auth.initialize_earth_engine
documentation:
Initializes the Earth Engine Python API

This function is required to be called before using the Earth Engine Python API.

documentation: noqa: E501

classes.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetDefinition
documentation: Holds all additional values required to reconstruct the dataset

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetBandClass.default_color
documentation: A hex color string, e.g. FF0000, that is used by default to represent this class

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetBand.center_wavelength
documentation: Center wavelength of the band, in micrometers.

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetBand.full_width_half_max
documentation: Full width of the band at half maximum transmission, in micrometers.

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetBand.sensor_ground_sample_distance
documentation:
Ground sample distance of the sensor, in meters.

This is not the pixel size. The actual pixel size depends on geometry,
wavelength and processing steps.

See https://github.com/radiantearth/stac-spec/blob/master/commons/common-metadata.md#gsd

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetBand.common_name
documentation:
Common name of the band, e.g. "red", "nir"

See https://github.com/stac-extensions/eo?tab=readme-ov-file#common-band-names

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetBand.classes
documentation: For categorical bands, mapping of values to semantic classes

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.TemporalResolution.value
documentation: The number of units in the interval

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.TemporalResolution.type
documentation:
How the interval should be interpreted. Depends on the data source. For example,
"cadence" means that the interval is the time between two consecutive data points
in a time series. "revisit_interval" means that the interval is the time between
two consecutive observations of the same location (not necessarily reflected in the
time series). "climatological_interval" means that the interval is the time over
which the data is aggregated to create a statistical summary.

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.TemporalResolution.unit
documentation: The time unit of the interval

type.name: dict[str, tuple[float | None, float | None]] | None

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetMetadata.supports_custom_viz

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetMetadata.thumbnail_url
documentation: If available, publically accessible URL to a thumbnail image

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetMetadata.license
documentation: License as a short unique key, e.g. "CC-BY-SA-4.0"

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetMetadata.terms_of_use
documentation:
More detailed terms of use, e.g. a URL to a page with the full license text
or just an explanation of the license

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetMetadata.providers
documentation:
Source of the dataset, e.g. the organization that produced the data,
the organization that hosts the data, etc.

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetMetadata.temporal_extent
documentation:
Temporal extent of the dataset.

This is the start and end time of data contained in this dataset.
If there is no time axis, this is the source time of the pixels in the dataset.

If the dataset is a time series, this is the start and end time of the time series.

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetMetadata.ground_sample_distance
documentation:
Ground sample distance of the dataset, in meters.

This is not the pixel size. The actual pixel size depends on geometry,
wavelength and processing steps.

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetMetadata.temporal_resolution
documentation:
Temporal resolution of the dataset, if it is a time series.

This is provided for information only, check the labels of the time series for
the actual time resolution. For example, there might be missing data points.

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetMetadata.platform
documentation: Platform(s) used to collect the data, e.g. "Sentinel-2A"

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetMetadata.instrument
documentation: Instrument(s) used to collect the data, e.g. "MSI"

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetMetadata.source_id
documentation: If set, this identifies the dataset in the source system (e.g. Earth Engine)

classes.name: earthscale.datasets.dataset.DatasetDomain
documentation: Defines which type of catalog a dataset belongs to.

Only one of the three will be set.

functions.name: earthscale.datasets._earthengine._parse_ee_wavelength_info
documentation:
Fills in missing center wavelength and FWHM from GEE wavelength field,
if available and parseable.

The GEE wavelength field contains strings, which can be formatted like (at least):

- `2.360-2.430μm`
- `740.2nm (S2A) / 739.1nm (S2B)`
- `925.4100nm, FWHM: 11.2754nm`
- `10.40 - 12.50 μm`
- `620-670nm`
- `858nm`
- `2130nm/2105 - 2155nm`
- `0.402 - 0.422µm`
- `0.846-0.885µm`

documentation: Will return the region from a GCS URL

documentation: Using a lower `maxsize` as the images are potentially quite large

functions.name: earthscale.datasets.raster._update_min_max_metadata
documentation: Updates min/max values if both are present in the band info

functions.name: earthscale.datasets.raster._guess_crs_from_coords
documentation:
Guess the CRS based on x/y coordinate ranges.

VERY rough guess based on coordinate ranges.

Args:
    x: Array of x coordinates
    y: Array of y coordinates

Returns:
    Guessed CRS (defaults to EPSG:4326 if unable to determine)

Common cases:
    - EPSG:4326 (WGS84): x: [-180, 180], y: [-90, 90]
    - EPSG:3857 (Web Mercator): x/y: [-20037508.34, 20037508.34]

functions.name: earthscale.datasets.raster._get_raster_info
documentation:
Get raster metadata.

see: https://github.com/stac-extensions/raster#raster-band-object
Copied from rio-stac
https://github.com/developmentseed/rio-stac/blob/52a13eec0c8ad19dee904b2bc0cd529b73b95899/rio_stac/stac.py#
but removed stats creation for performance reasons as it takes too long for rasters
with a lot of bands and we don't use it yet

functions.name: earthscale.datasets.raster.reshape_dset_to_band_info
documentation:
ODC STAC output dataset originally has one data variable per datetime+band.
This function reshapes it to have one data variable per band, with
datetimes as coordinates.

classes.name: earthscale.datasets.raster.DatasetCache
documentation:
Geo-aware cache for datasets, checking to see if we already
have a dataset with the same bounding box and bands.

Evicts the oldest entries first

classes.name: earthscale.datasets.raster.ZarrDataset
documentation:
Dataset based on a Zarr store.

When loading into xarray, this dataset type will automatically standardize the
dimensions of the dataset to 'y', 'x' and 'time' if present. It will try to infer
spatial dimensions, so if 'lon' or 'longitude' is present, it will be renamed to
'x'.

This only supports datasets with 2 or 3 dimensions. If an additional dimension like
'band' is present, it will be renamed to 'band_1', 'band_2' etc. If more than one
additional dimension is present, a `ValueError` will be raised.

Args:
    store:
        The Zarr store to load the dataset from.
    name:
        The name of the dataset. Defaults to a random UUID. If explicitly given, the
        dataset will be visible in the Earthscale platform.
    rename:
        A dictionary mapping the original dimension names to the desired dimension
        names.
    kwargs:
        Additional keyword arguments to pass to `xarray.open_zarr`.

classes.name: earthscale.datasets.raster.STACDataset
documentation:
Spatio-Temporal Asset Catalog (STAC) based dataset

Args:
    items:
        Items to build the dataset from. We allow passing in serialized stac items
        (dicts) as well.
        If no explicit geobox is passed in, the geobox will be determined from the
        items. In this case, the proj extension to the STAC item is required. When
        using rio-stac's `create_stac_item` function, this can be achieved by
        passing in the `with_proj=True` argument.

    bands:
        List of bands to load. Defaults to all bands

    groupby:
        Controls what items get placed in to the same pixel plane.

        The following have special meaning:

           * "time" items with exactly the same timestamp are grouped together
           * "solar_day" items captured on the same day adjusted for solar time
           * "id" every item is loaded separately

        Any other string is assumed to be a key in Item's properties dictionary.
        Please note that contrary to `odc.stac.load` we do not support callables as
        we need to be able to serialize the dataset.

    name:
        Name of the dataset. Defaults to a random UUID. If explicitly given,
        The dataset will be visible in the Earthscale platform

    metadata:
        Dataset Metadata. Defaults to None.

    kwargs:
        Additional keyword arguments to pass to
        [`odc.stac.load`](https://odc-stac.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_api/odc.stac.load.html)
        Only serializable arguments can be passed to STAC.

class_variables.name: earthscale.datasets.raster.BandInfoRow.index
documentation: 0-based band index

classes.name: earthscale.datasets.raster.ImageDataset
documentation:
Dataset based on single images.

 Images must be in a format that can be read by `rasterio`. Under the hood, an
 `ImageDataset` creates a list of STAC items and then uses `odc.stac.load` to load
 the dataset.

 An important concept here is how the time dimension of the `Dataset` is set and
 which images account for which time.

 To group images by time (and thus create a time dimension), you can use the
 `groupby` argument. For more information on the options, please refer to the
 documentation of `odc.stac.load`
 ([here](https://odc-stac.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_api/odc.stac.load.html)). Note
 that as we need to serialize the class, we do not allow callables to be passed in.

 As images generally don't have time information, we provide several ways to set the
 time interval for an image. Only one of the following options can be set:

 1. `band_info`: A pandas DataFrame with band information. This is used to parse
    TIFF band indices into band name and datetime information.
 2. `datetime_`: Either a single `datetime.datetime` or a tuple of two
    `datetime.datetime` objects. If a single `datetime.datetime` is provided, all
    images will have the same timestamp. If a tuple is provided, the first element
    will be the start time and the second element will be the end time. This
    interval will be used for all images. This will result in all images having the
    same time interval.

Args:
   glob_url:
       URL pattern to find images with. E.g. `gs://bucket/path/to/images/*.tif`.
   bands:
       List of bands to load. Defaults to all bands.
   band_info:
       DataFrame with band information. Defaults to None. This is used to provide
       metadata about bands in the images. It maps the band index to the band name
       and optionally time, min and max values. The index of the dataframe should be
       the band index (0-indexed). The following columns can be present:
         - name (str; required): The name of the band
         - datetime (datetime.datetime; optional): The datetime of the band
         - min (float; optional): The minimum value of the band
         - max (float; optional): The maximum value of the band
       We also allow this to be passed in as a dictionary of
       {column -> {index -> value}}.
       Can only be used if `datetime_` is not set.
   filename_date_pattern:
        A string pattern representing how dates are formatted in the filenames.
        This pattern uses strftime-style format codes to extract date information.

        Common format codes:
        %Y - Year with century as a decimal number (e.g., 2023)
        %m - Month as a zero-padded decimal number (01-12)
        %d - Day of the month as a zero-padded decimal number (01-31)

        Example:
        - For files named like "brasil_coverage_2011.tif":
          filename_date_pattern="%Y"

        If None (default), no date information will be extracted from filenames.
   filename_band_pattern:
        A dictionary mapping wildcard band name patterns to actual band names.
        E.g. {"*_B[0-9]": "band_1"} would map all bands starting with "B" and
        ending with a number to "band_1". Uses Unix filename pattern rules.
   groupby:
        Controls what items get placed in to the same pixel plane.

        The following have special meaning:

           * "time" items with exactly the same timestamp are grouped together
           * "solar_day" items captured on the same day adjusted for solar time
           * "id" every item is loaded separately

        Any other string is assumed to be a key in Item's properties dictionary.
        Please note that contrary to `odc.stac.load` we do not support callables as
        we need to be able to serialize the dataset.
   datetime_:
       Either a single `datetime.datetime` or a tuple of two `datetime.datetime`
       objects. If a single `datetime.datetime` is provided, all images will have
       the same time. If a tuple is provided, the first element will be the start
       time and the second element will be the end time. This interval will be
       valid for all images.
       Can only be set if `band_info` is not set.
   name:
       Name of the dataset. Defaults to a random UUID. If explicitly given, the
       dataset will visible in the Earthscale platform
   metadata:
       Dataset Metadata. Defaults to None.
   kwargs:
       Additional keyword arguments to pass to `odc.stac.load`
       (more information in
       [their docs](https://odc-stac.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_api/odc.stac.load.html))

classes.name: earthscale.datasets.raster.EarthEngineDataset

methods.name: earthscale.datasets.raster.EarthEngineDataset.from_earth_engine_catalog
documentation:
Load an Earth Engine dataset from the Earth Engine catalog.

The id can be found in the "Collection Snippet" field.
Example value: "COPERNICUS/DEM/GLO30"

methods.name: earthscale.datasets.raster.EarthEngineDataset.get_dates
documentation:
Auto-guesses useful dates from the time range of the metadata.

Logic:
if we have > 10 years, use years,
if we just have one year, use months,
if we have less than half a year use weeks
if we have less than a month use days

TODO: figure out how temporal_resolution should play into this

Load data from Earth Engine.

Args:
    image_collection:
        Either an ee.ImageCollection object or JSON dict of the image collection
    quality_band:
        The band used to determine the pixel ordering in the mosaic creation. Please
        refer to the [Earth-engine docs](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/apidocs/ee-imagecollection-qualitymosaic)
        for an example.
    viz_params:
        Visualization parameters for the mosaic. Please refer to the docs on
        [Image Visualization](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/guides/image_visualization)
        on what's possible here.

        An example here would be:
        ```python
        viz_params = {
            "bands": ["B4", "B3", "B2"],
            "min": [0, 0, 0],
            "max": [0.3, 0.3, 0.3]
        }
        ```

    name:
        The name of the dataset. Defaults to a random UUID. If explicitly given, the
        dataset will be visible in the Earthscale platform.

classes.name: earthscale.datasets.raster.TileServerDataset
documentation:
Load data from a tile server.

Args:
    url (str): The URL of the tile server with x, y, z placeholders, e.g.
        `https://server.com/tiles/{z}/{x}/{y}.png`.
    name (str, optional): The name of the dataset. Defaults to a random UUID.
        If explicitly given, the dataset will be visible in the Earthscale platform.
